examples.text: وَأَمْدَدْنَاكُمْ بِأَمْوَالٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ أَكْثَرَ نَفِيرًا
examples.english: They said, “We would give you the upper hand over them and aid you with wealth and offspring, causing you to outnumber them.”

body.text: an inf. n. used as a subst.: (Msb:) or
a people going to execute an affair:
a people going with one to fight;
: (M, K:) each is a noun having a pl. signification: (M:) or the first and last signify
and the pl. of each is
those of a man's people who go forth with him to war:
or it is a pl. [or quasi-pl.] of
men assembled to go to the enemy:
The company of the sons of such a one, that came forth to execute an affair, arrived.
Those of Kureysh who went forth to Bedr
to defend the caravan of Aboo-Sufyán, (M,) which was coming from Syria. (T.) Hence the proverb,
Such a one is neither in the caravan nor in the company going forth to fight

]: applied to him who is not regarded as fit for a difficult undertaking: because none held back from the caravan and the fight except him who was crippled by disease and him in whom was no good; (TA:) or the original words of the proverb are

body.text_bw: lA fiY AlEiyri walaA fiY Aln~afiyri
body.text_ar: لا فِى العِيرِ وَلَا فِى النَّفِيرِ

body.text: : and these words were first said by Aboo-Sufyán, with reference to the Benoo-Zuhrah, when he found them turning back towards Mekkeh; and, accord. to As, are applied to a man who is held in low and little repute. (Mgh.) [See also Freytag's Arab. Prov., ii. 500.]
